background: 94 sources in corpus-batch-v4 with no_search_results and has_js=false (per corpus-manifest). Investigated via reader-cli --test-source with --record (full L2-search HTTP evidence captured for all 94).
methodology: Extracted 94 no-JS no_search_results sources from reports/tooling/corpus-batch-v4.json
Ran reader-cli --test-source on ALL 94 with --keyword "斗破苍穹" --timeout 15 --record
Captured L2-search HTTP request URL, status, response body, final_url for each source
Inspected each source ruleSearch.bookList and searchUrl rules

Classified by HTTP outcome + body markers + rule syntax; refined to avoid false positives (weak anti-crawl markers like "请登录"/"验证码" only count in small challenge pages <3KB, since large legitimate search pages often contain nav-bar login links)

category_definitions.no_results: HTTP 200, site responded normally but search genuinely returned 0 books (empty-state markers / empty JSON array / no-results page). Normal behavior, not a bug.
category_definitions.anti_crawl: HTTP 403/429, Cloudflare challenge, captcha, login/verify redirect, or man-machine verification. Site blocks automated requests. Not fixable in Core rules.
category_definitions.website_dead: Domain unreachable, server error (5xx), 404, redirect to empty/stub page, or site migrated/shut down (stopweb). Not fixable.
category_definitions.rule_bug: HTTP 200 with substantial response, but Reader Core failed to extract books due to a rule-engine bug or unsupported/outdated rule syntax. Actionable.
category_definitions.encoding: Response body decoding error (charset mismatch). Not observed in this batch.

has_js_misclassification.description: corpus-manifest has_js flag is wrong for 27/94 sources in this set. The classifier (tools/corpus-manager/corpus_manager.py detect_rule_forms L61-109) only checks rule_str.startswith("@js:"), missing @js: that appears mid-rule or after a newline (Legado "prefix@js:transform" pattern). has_js_search (L132) only checks searchUrl for @js:, not rule fields. 5 of the 27 have JS in ruleSearch.* (affects L2 path): 网易云说(bookList), 荔枝音乐(bookUrl), 荔枝有声(bookUrl), 纵横中文(kind), 酷我小说(kind).
has_js_misclassification.fix: In detect_rule_forms, also check ("@js:" in rule_str) and ("<js>" in rule_str), not just startswith. Or scan all rule field values for @js:/<js> substrings when computing has_js.

url_construction_bug.description: Reader Core joins bookSourceUrl + relative searchUrl via STRING CONCATENATION, not RFC 3986 URL resolution. When bookSourceUrl contains a # fragment, the fragment is not stripped, producing a malformed URL where the search path becomes part of the fragment (e.g. "http://m.yuedu.163.com#yc1101" + "/search/book/data.json" -> "http://m.yuedu.163.com#yc1101/search/book/data.json" — the server sees path "/" with fragment "yc1101/search/..." and returns the homepage). These # suffixes (e.g. #yc1101, #♤yc, #一程, #按钮筛选) are Legado source labels/tags used for grouping in the Legado UI; Legado strips them before building request URLs, but Reader Core does not. All 7 affected sources verified by hand: the # originates from bookSourceUrl in every case (not from searchUrl).
functions: url_origin (L907-915): rest.split("/").next() only splits on "/", should split on ["/","?","#"]
url_construction_bug.fix: Use rest.split(["/", "?", "#"]).next() in url_origin, OR adopt the url crate Url::parse(base).join(relative) for proper RFC 3986 resolution. No test currently covers base_url with a # fragment. This single fix would likely recover 7 of the 94 sources (the url_fragment_not_stripped subset).

bookList_rule_mismatch.description: Largest actionable category. HTTP 200 with a real search-results or homepage body, but Reader Core extracted 0 books. Two sub-causes: (a) bookList rule references CSS classes that no longer exist in the HTML (site redesigned), (b) bookList rule uses @js: transform that Reader Core does not support (and which the manifest misclassified as no-JS).
bookList_rule_mismatch.recommended_next_step: For each rule_bug source, diff ruleSearch.bookList against the recorded L2-search HTML body (in /tmp/test-source-records*/corpus-<id>.json) to determine whether the rule is outdated (site redesign) or uses unsupported syntax. Sites that redesigned need updated rules (out of scope for Core); unsupported syntax needs Core rule-engine work.
